question: Explain Riverpod: providers, WidgetRef, and AsyncNotifier.
answer:
Riverpod is a complete Provider rewrite with compile-time safety.

Key advantages over Provider:
• No context needed to read providers outside widgets
• Compile-time type safety — no runtime ProviderNotFoundException
• Auto-dispose — no memory leaks from forgotten providers
• DevTools integration — view provider state

Provider types:
• Provider<T> — read-only derived value
• StateProvider<T> — simple mutable state
• NotifierProvider — complex sync state (Notifier class)
• AsyncNotifierProvider — async state with loading/error/data
• StreamProvider — stream of values
• FutureProvider — one-time async value

question: Explain Riverpod family modifier and how to override providers in tests.
answer:
family — parametrize a provider by an argument. Creates a separate provider instance per parameter value. Each instance is cached and auto-disposed when no longer watched.

Use for: fetching by ID, filtering by category, locale-specific providers.

Testing with Riverpod — override any provider in ProviderScope for unit and widget tests. This replaces the real implementation with a mock.

question: Interview scenario #20: How would you handle code review readiness for release rollback plan in a Flutter app?
answer:
Submit small focused changes, document non-obvious decisions, and include tests that prove behavior before and after.

Focus area: release rollback plan. In interviews, explain trade-offs, failure modes, and how you would validate the result in production.

question: Interview scenario #43: How would you handle state management trade-offs for navigation guards in a Flutter app?
answer:
Choose the simplest solution that fits scale: local state for small screens, Provider/Riverpod/BLoC for shared or asynchronous flows.

Focus area: navigation guards. In interviews, explain trade-offs, failure modes, and how you would validate the result in production.

question: Interview scenario #66: How would you handle API integration for pagination strategy in a Flutter app?
answer:
Model API responses with typed DTOs, validate nullable fields, and centralize request/retry logic in a data layer.

Focus area: pagination strategy. In interviews, explain trade-offs, failure modes, and how you would validate the result in production.

question: Interview scenario #89: How would you handle stream handling for dependency injection in a Flutter app?
answer:
Cancel subscriptions in dispose, debounce noisy sources, and keep transformation logic in dedicated services or blocs.

Focus area: dependency injection. In interviews, explain trade-offs, failure modes, and how you would validate the result in production.
